walkthrough.title: Choose a Scenario
walkthrough.text: Select one of the available scenario sheets. Each scenario tells a different Lovecraftian horror story with unique objectives, monsters, and challenges. The scenario determines the map layout, event cards, monster mix, and mythos tokens you'll use. Return unused scenarios to the box.

walkthrough.title: Build the Board
walkthrough.text: The back of the scenario sheet shows a setup map. Place neighborhood tiles and street tiles as indicated. Each neighborhood has a central area and surrounding spaces. Streets connect neighborhoods. Return excess tiles to the box.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Decks and Monster Cup
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the event cards for your scenario to create the event deck (encounter text face down). Take the indicated monster cards and shuffle them into the monster deck (ready side up). Place the scenario's mythos tokens into an opaque container to create the mythos cup. Create the headline deck from 13 random headline cards.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Assets and Display
walkthrough.text: Shuffle item, ally, and spell cards into separate asset decks. Reveal 5 item cards to create the display — this is the shop where investigators can buy equipment. Keep special cards alphabetized and condition cards nearby for easy access.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Token Pools and Archive
walkthrough.text: Separate all tokens by type (damage, horror, clues/doom, money, remnants) and keep them accessible. Keep all archive cards in numerical order nearby — these numbered cards drive the story and can be added to the codex during play.

walkthrough.title: Choose Investigators
walkthrough.text: Each player selects an investigator sheet. Take your investigator token, one activation token (one player gets the leader token instead), and a reference card. Each investigator has unique stats, a special ability, and a choice of starting possessions — look at both options before choosing.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Elements
walkthrough.text: Place investigators on their starting spaces as shown on the scenario sheet. Spawn 3 clues by placing clue tokens in neighborhoods corresponding to the top event deck cards. Spread doom once by placing doom tokens as indicated. Then resolve the scenario's 'finalize setup' instructions and read the opening codex cards.

walkthrough.title: Read the Codex
walkthrough.text: The codex includes the scenario sheet and any archive cards added during setup. Read all cards — they explain the story, provide the win and loss conditions, and set up any special rules for this scenario. The clue icon points toward winning; the doom icon points toward losing. You're ready to face the horrors of Arkham!

walkthrough.title: Round Overview
walkthrough.text: Each round has four phases in order: Action, Monster, Encounter, and Mythos. After all four phases, a new round begins. The game continues until investigators win (by progressing codex cards positively) or lose (too much doom, or all investigators eliminated).

walkthrough.title: Action Phase — Your Turn
walkthrough.text: Investigators take turns in any order they choose. On your turn, perform up to 2 actions. Available actions: Move (to adjacent space), Attack (test strength against a monster), Evade (test observation to disengage), Focus (improve a skill), Ward (test lore to remove doom), Research (spend clues from scenario sheet), Trade (exchange items with adjacent investigator), or Component actions from your cards.

walkthrough.title: Engaged with Monsters
walkthrough.text: If you're in the same space as a monster and it engages you, your action options are restricted to Attack, Evade, Focus, and specifically allowed actions. You cannot move away, resolve encounters, or do most other things until you evade or defeat the monster. Engagement is the primary danger in Arkham Horror.

walkthrough.title: Tests and Dice
walkthrough.text: Many actions require tests — rolling dice equal to a skill value. Each 5 or 6 is a success. The number of successes needed depends on the test. You can spend clues to reroll dice, and the Focus action lets you add dice to future tests. Modifiers from cards, monsters, and conditions can increase or decrease your dice pool.

walkthrough.title: Monster Phase
walkthrough.text: Three steps: (1) Monster Activation — each ready monster activates by resolving its activation text, usually moving toward prey (investigators). A monster that moves into an investigator's space engages them. (2) Monster Attack — each engaged monster deals its damage and horror values to the investigator. (3) Monster Ready — exhausted monsters ready and may engage nearby investigators.

walkthrough.title: Damage and Horror
walkthrough.text: Damage reduces health; horror reduces sanity. When you suffer damage or horror, place tokens on your investigator sheet. You can assign some damage/horror to your assets (allies, items with health/sanity) instead. If your damage equals your health OR horror equals your sanity, you're defeated — discard your possessions and choose a new investigator next round.

walkthrough.title: Encounter Phase
walkthrough.text: Each investigator draws and resolves an encounter card matching their space type (neighborhood or street). Encounters present narrative choices and skill tests. If you're in a neighborhood with a clue, the encounter may let you gain that clue. If you're engaged with a monster, you skip your encounter — another reason to deal with monsters quickly.

walkthrough.title: Mythos Phase
walkthrough.text: Each investigator draws 2 tokens from the mythos cup and resolves them one at a time. Token types: Spread Doom (place doom on the board), Spawn Monster (add a monster), Spawn Clue (place a clue and shuffle event cards), Read Headline (resolve headline card), Gate Burst (doom in a neighborhood), Reckoning (resolve all reckoning effects), or Blank (nothing). This is how the game escalates.

walkthrough.title: Doom and the Losing Path
walkthrough.text: Doom accumulates on the board through mythos tokens and monster effects. When a space has 3 doom or a neighborhood has 5 total doom, an anomaly token appears. Additional doom in anomaly neighborhoods goes on the scenario sheet instead. Too much doom on the scenario sheet progresses the codex negatively — eventually causing you to lose.

walkthrough.title: Clues and the Winning Path
walkthrough.text: Clues spawn when Spawn Clue mythos tokens are drawn, and event cards get shuffled into the top of neighborhood decks. When you encounter a neighborhood with clues, you may gain those clues. Use the Research action to place clues from your supply onto the scenario sheet, progressing the codex positively toward victory.

walkthrough.title: The Codex and Archive
walkthrough.text: The codex is the living story of the game. It starts with the scenario sheet and grows as archive cards are added. Cards with clue icons show what you need to do to win. Cards with doom icons show what will happen if things go badly. Read new codex cards carefully — they add rules, change conditions, and drive the narrative.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: There's no default way to win — you must progress the codex by gaining and researching clues until a card says you win. You lose if doom causes negative codex progression to a loss condition, or if every available investigator is defeated/devoured. The tension between gathering clues (winning) and managing doom (not losing) is the heart of the game.

Win by codex progression; lose by doom or total defeat

walkthrough.title: Setting the Scene
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through the first round of a 2-player game. Alex plays Roland Banks (the Fed, strong in combat) starting at Northside. Blake plays Agnes Baker (the Waitress, strong in lore) starting at Rivertown. The scenario has placed doom tokens and one monster — a Ghoul in Easttown.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase — Roland's First Action: Move
walkthrough.text: Roland wants to reach Easttown where the Ghoul lurks near a clue. He uses his first action to Move from Northside through the connecting street to Easttown. There's a clue in Easttown's central area and the Ghoul is in an adjacent space.

walkthrough.title: Roland's Second Action: Attack
walkthrough.text: Roland attacks the Ghoul. The Ghoul's attack modifier is -1, so Roland rolls his strength (4) minus 1 = 3 dice. He rolls: 3, 5, 6. That's 2 successes! The Ghoul has 2 health, so it's defeated. Roland gains a remnant from the Ghoul's remnant icon.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase — Agnes's First Action: Focus
walkthrough.text: Agnes uses Focus as her first action, choosing to improve her lore skill. She places a focus token, which will give her an extra die on her next lore test. She's preparing for the encounter phase where she'll need strong lore checks.

walkthrough.title: Agnes's Second Action: Ward
walkthrough.text: Agnes uses Ward to try removing doom from Rivertown. She tests lore (3 + 1 focus = 4 dice). She rolls: 2, 5, 5, 6. Three successes! She removes one doom token from her space. Every doom removed helps slow the path to losing.

walkthrough.title: Player Mistake — Forgetting Action Limits
walkthrough.text: Agnes wants to also Move to reach a clue, but she's already used both actions this turn. Remember: exactly 2 actions per turn during the Action phase. Each action type can only be performed once per round (you can't Attack twice, even on different turns in the same round).

walkthrough.title: Monster Phase — No Monsters Left
walkthrough.text: Roland defeated the only monster on the board, so the Monster Phase is quick: no activations, no attacks, no readying needed. If monsters had been present, they would move toward their prey (usually the nearest investigator), then engaged ones would deal damage and horror.

walkthrough.title: Encounter Phase — Roland's Encounter
walkthrough.text: Roland is in Easttown with a clue. He draws a neighborhood encounter card. It reads: 'You notice strange symbols scratched into the alley wall. Test lore. If you pass, gain a clue from your neighborhood.' Roland tests lore (2 dice) and rolls: 4, 6. One success — he passes! He gains the clue from Easttown.

walkthrough.title: Player Mistake — Ignoring Clue Strategy
walkthrough.text: Roland gained a clue, but some new players forget to use the Research action to actually place clues on the scenario sheet. Holding clues is useful for rerolling dice, but you NEED to research them to win. Plan turns around both gaining and researching clues.

walkthrough.title: Encounter Phase — Agnes's Encounter
walkthrough.text: Agnes draws a Rivertown encounter: 'A strange woman offers you a book. You may gain a spell.' Agnes gains a spell card from the spell deck. Spells are powerful but often have risks — this one lets her deal damage using lore instead of strength, perfect for her build.

walkthrough.title: Mythos Phase — Roland Draws Tokens
walkthrough.text: Roland (the leader) draws first. Token 1: Spawn Clue — a new clue appears in the neighborhood matching the top event card. Token 2: Blank — no effect. Relatively lucky draw! The clue spawn also shuffled event cards into the top of a neighborhood deck, seeding future encounters.

walkthrough.title: Mythos Phase — Agnes Draws Tokens
walkthrough.text: Agnes draws Token 1: Spread Doom — doom is placed on the board (one in each space of the neighborhood matching the scenario's doom rules). Token 2: Spawn Monster — a new monster is drawn from the monster deck and placed per its spawn rules. The threats are building. End of Round 1.

walkthrough.title: Ready for Round 2
walkthrough.text: After one round, you've seen all four phases in action. Roland gained a clue and defeated a monster. Agnes warded doom and gained a spell. But doom spread and a new monster appeared. This push-and-pull continues every round. Next, plan how to research clues while managing the growing threats. The codex will guide you toward your objective!

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Clues Over Combat
walkthrough.text: Clues are how you win. Combat is how you survive. New players often spend too many actions fighting monsters while doom accumulates. Evade monsters when possible to save actions for encounters and research. Only fight when a monster blocks critical progress or when defeat grants useful remnants.

walkthrough.title: Specialize Your Investigators
walkthrough.text: Don't try to make every investigator do everything. High-strength investigators should handle monsters; high-lore investigators should ward doom and resolve tricky encounters. High-observation investigators are great at evading. Play to your stats.

walkthrough.title: Manage the Display
walkthrough.text: When you can't or don't want to buy from the display, you can discard up to 2 cards and draw replacements. Use this to cycle bad items away and find gear that matches your investigator's strengths. Don't let a stale display sit for multiple rounds.

walkthrough.title: Coordinate Movement
walkthrough.text: Stay close enough to trade items and support each other in combat, but spread out enough to cover multiple neighborhoods for encounters. The trade action is invaluable — pass items to whoever can use them best.

walkthrough.title: Use Clues for Rerolls Wisely
walkthrough.text: Clues can be spent to reroll dice on any test, which is powerful. But every clue spent on rerolls is one fewer clue to research for the win. Save rerolls for critical tests — evading a dangerous monster, or passing a test that would progress the codex.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Doom Clock
walkthrough.text: Track how much doom is on the board and scenario sheet. If doom is accumulating fast, prioritize the Ward action and anomaly management. If doom is manageable, focus on clue gathering. Knowing the doom state helps you decide when to be aggressive vs. defensive.

walkthrough.title: Read Your Codex Cards Carefully
walkthrough.text: Codex cards contain critical information about how to win and what triggers losses. Some have reckoning effects that fire every time a reckoning token is drawn from the mythos cup. Missing these effects is a common mistake that can snowball against you.

summary.title: Prioritize Clues Over Combat
bullets: Clues = winning; Combat = surviving
Evade when possible to save actions
Only fight when blocking progress or for remnants

summary.title: Specialize Your Investigators
bullets: High strength → combat
High lore → ward and encounters
Play to your stats, don't generalize

summary.title: Manage the Display
bullets: Discard up to 2 cards when not buying
Cycle stale items for better options
Match gear to investigator strengths

summary.title: Coordinate Movement
bullets: Stay close for trades and combat support

Trade action passes items to best user

summary.title: Use Clues for Rerolls Wisely

Re-read after each codex addition

walkthrough.title: Mythos Cup Probability Management
walkthrough.text: The mythos cup is NOT refilled until empty. Track which tokens have been drawn — if both Spread Doom tokens are out, the cup is safer until it refills. Plan aggressive clue runs during 'safe' mythos phases and defensive play when dangerous tokens remain.

walkthrough.title: Anomaly Control
walkthrough.text: Anomalies appear when doom concentrates (3 in a space or 5 in a neighborhood). They redirect excess doom to the scenario sheet — accelerating your loss. Ward proactively in neighborhoods approaching the threshold. Sometimes it's better to spread doom across multiple neighborhoods than let one spike.

walkthrough.title: Event Card Deck Manipulation
walkthrough.text: When clues spawn, event cards get shuffled into the top of neighborhood decks. This means neighborhoods with clues have event encounters near the top. Prioritize encountering in these neighborhoods — event encounters often give the best clue-gaining opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Focus Token Economy
walkthrough.text: Focus tokens are the most efficient way to boost test results. Stack 2-3 focus tokens on your primary skill before a critical test. One focus action is often worth more than buying an item that gives +1 to a skill. Plan focus turns before big encounter or research rounds.

walkthrough.title: Monster Engagement Tactics
walkthrough.text: When a monster engages you, you can choose to attack a different monster in your space instead (engaging it away from another player). Use this to protect fragile investigators. Also, attacking an exhausted monster doesn't engage it — free damage without commitment.

walkthrough.title: Research Timing
walkthrough.text: Don't research clues one at a time — it costs an action each time. Stockpile 2-3 clues, then research in one burst. This is more action-efficient. Exception: if the codex shows you're one clue away from a major progression, research immediately to advance the story.

summary.title: Mythos Cup Probability
bullets: Cup not refilled until empty
Track drawn tokens — safe vs dangerous phases
Plan aggressive play during safe cups

summary.title: Anomaly Control
bullets: Anomalies: 3 doom in space or 5 in neighborhood

Ward proactively, spread doom across neighborhoods

summary.title: Event Deck Manipulation

summary.title: Focus Token Economy

summary.title: Research Timing

entries.definition: A player character with unique stats (health, sanity, skills), a special ability, and starting possessions. Each player controls one investigator.
The collection of the scenario sheet and added archive cards that drives the game's story, provides win/loss conditions, and adds special rules.
Numbered cards kept in order that are added to the codex or used during play as directed by the scenario and game effects.
Tokens representing the encroaching cosmic horror. Placed on spaces, neighborhoods, and the scenario sheet. Too much doom on the scenario sheet leads to losing.
Tokens gained from encounters that can be spent to reroll dice or placed on the scenario sheet via the Research action to progress toward winning.
First phase of each round. Investigators take turns performing up to 2 actions each in any player order.
Second phase. Monsters activate (move toward prey), attack (deal damage/horror to engaged investigators), then ready (exhausted monsters flip ready).
Third phase. Each investigator draws and resolves an encounter card matching their space. Skipped if engaged with a monster.
Fourth phase. Each investigator draws 2 tokens from the mythos cup and resolves their effects (doom, monsters, clues, headlines, etc.).
Move your investigator to an adjacent space (connected by a border).
Test strength against a monster in your space. Each success deals 1 damage. The monster's attack modifier adjusts your dice.
Test observation to disengage from a monster. If successful, the monster exhausts and you're free.
Place a focus token to gain +1 die on a future test of a chosen skill.
Test lore to remove doom from your space. Each success removes 1 doom.
Place clues from your supply onto the scenario sheet to progress the codex toward victory.
Exchange items, clues, money, or remnants with an investigator in your space or adjacent space.
Unique actions printed on investigator sheets or asset cards. Each can be performed once per round.
A monster in your space that is actively threatening you. Limits your available actions to Attack, Evade, and Focus.
A monster state where it's active and will activate during the Monster Phase. Ready monsters engage investigators in their space.
A monster state after being evaded. Exhausted monsters don't activate or attack, but ready at the end of the Monster Phase.
The target a monster moves toward during activation. Usually the nearest investigator, but some monsters have specific prey.
A token placed in a neighborhood when doom concentrates (3 in a space or 5 total). Redirects future doom to the scenario sheet.
The 5 face-up item cards available for purchase. Can be cycled by discarding up to 2 when not buying.
Opaque container with scenario-specific tokens drawn during the Mythos Phase. Not refilled until empty.
Cards drawn when a Read Headline mythos token appears. Provide narrative events and game effects.
Tokens placed on investigators or assets representing physical harm. Investigator defeated when damage equals health.
Tokens placed on investigators or assets representing mental trauma. Investigator defeated when horror equals sanity.
An investigator whose damage equals health OR horror equals sanity. They discard possessions and the player chooses a new investigator next round.
Collective term for allies, items, spells, conditions, and talents in an investigator's possession.
A type of asset representing a status affecting an investigator (blessed, cursed, injured, etc.).
A token gained from defeating certain monsters. Used for various game effects depending on the scenario.
A mythos token that triggers all reckoning effects on the scenario sheet, codex cards, and investigators' cards simultaneously.
A large tile with a central area and surrounding spaces. Encounters are drawn from neighborhood-specific decks.
Connecting tiles between neighborhoods. Have their own encounter deck.
Two spaces sharing a border. Important for movement, trading, and monster engagement.
The investigator with the leader token. Goes first during the Mythos Phase and resolves tie-breaking decisions.
Mythos token effect: place doom tokens according to the scenario's doom-spreading rules.
Mythos token effect: draw a monster from the monster deck and place it per its spawn rules.
Mythos token effect: place a clue in the neighborhood matching the top event card, then shuffle cards into that deck.
Mythos token effect: place doom in each space of a neighborhood, then cycle the event deck.
